one.zh: 程序裁定:事实婚丈夫请求即决驳回事实婚妻子的财产/扶养申请,并口头申请就「事实关系终止日期」单独审理。法院两项均驳回——以最有利于妻子的方式看待其证据,她并非毫无胜诉前景;而单独审理「终止日期」并不窄、与众多重叠事实/可信度问题纠缠,不会显著省费或提前结案。法院并就推进单一专家证据作出若干程序命令。
one.en: A procedural ruling: the de facto husband sought summary dismissal of the de facto wife's property/maintenance claim and made an oral application for a separate hearing on the 'date of termination' of the relationship. The court refused both — taken at its highest, the wife's evidence did not leave her without reasonable prospects; and a separate trial of the termination date was not narrow, was entangled with overlapping facts and credit issues, and would not significantly save costs or hasten disposal. The court also made several procedural orders to progress the single-expert evidence.

issue.zh: 其一,事实婚丈夫能否取得即决驳回——即在以最有利于妻子的方式看待其证据后,她是否仍无合理胜诉前景?其二,是否应依规则第 10.10 条就「事实关系终止日期」(第 90RD 条宣告)单独审理?
issue.en: First, whether the de facto husband can obtain summary dismissal — i.e. whether, taking the wife's evidence at its highest, she has no reasonable prospects of success; second, whether a separate hearing under r 10.10 should be ordered on the date of termination of the de facto relationship (a s 90RD declaration).

subseq.zh: 即决驳回申请被驳回;单独审理「终止日期」的口头申请被驳回;第 90RD 条终止日期宣告及(如需)第 44(6) 条逾时许可,将与实体财产/扶养救济一并审理。丈夫 2026 年 3 月 31 日的程序申请因失去实益被驳回。法院授权妻子代丈夫签署专家聘任函,并命丈夫于 2026 年 5 月 14 日前向法务会计专家提交资料,逾期则其实体答辩被剔除、妻子可按无争辩方式进行。妻子要求丈夫预付 52,800 澳元专家费的请求被无损驳回(留待另案)。费用保留。
subseq.en: The summary-dismissal application was dismissed; the oral application for a separate hearing on the termination date was dismissed; the s 90RD termination-date declaration and any s 44(6) leave (if necessary) will be determined together with the substantive property/maintenance relief. The husband's 31 March 2026 procedural application was dismissed as having no utility. The court empowered the wife to execute the experts' engagement letters on the husband's behalf, and ordered the husband to provide documents to the forensic accountant by 14 May 2026, failing which his substantive response is struck out and the wife may proceed undefended. The wife's request that the husband pre-pay $52,800 in expert fees was dismissed without prejudice (left to a separate application). Costs were reserved.

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the de facto husband purports to seek summary judgment in his favour as against the de facto wife – Where the de facto husband’s contention that the determination of the date of the termination of the relationship is “dispositive” of the proceeding is not accepted – Where the de facto husband does not establish that the de facto wife has no reasonable prospects of success should her evidence be accepted at its highest – Application for summary dismissal refused. FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the de facto husband made an oral application for the listing of a separate issues hearing pursuant to r 10.10 of the Rules for a s 90RD declaration as to the date of termination of the de facto relationship – Where that factual issue is not narrow – Where the same witnesses will be called to give the same evidence at both possible hearings – Where the de facto husband has not established that the hearing of the separate issues trial would generate a significant saving of costs or lead to an earlier disposal of the proceeding – Oral application for a separate issues hearing dismissed. FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the de facto wife sought further procedural orders advancing ch 7 single expert evidence – Some orders made and others refused.

html.zh:
这是一宗事实婚(de facto)财产程序中的程序裁定。事实婚妻子 Ms Naumov 于 2023 年 12 月 20 日提起诉讼,依《家庭法法》第 90SM 条请求财产调整、第 90SE 条请求扶养;事实婚丈夫 Mr Geissler 答辩请求驳回,并主张妻子须依第 44(6) 条取得逾时许可。双方对「事实关系何时终止」存在核心争议:妻子称最终分居于 2021 年 12 月,丈夫称关系早在 2017 年 5 月或 2018 年 3 月即终止。

丈夫于 2026 年 1 月 30 日提出程序内申请,请求:认定最终分居日为 2017 年 5 月 29 日、驳回妻子的诉讼、并依第 90RD 条宣告双方此后并非事实关系(从而法院无管辖)。他主张「终止日期」是门槛性管辖问题、对全案具「决定性」——若终止于 2017/2018,则妻子的申请逾时、非经第 44(6) 条许可不能继续。这部分被理解为请求即决判决。

Campton 法官的处理。即决驳回:即决驳回的标准是「无合理胜诉前景」(第 102QAB 条、规则第 10.09 条),法院不解决争议事实,而是以最有利于申请人(此处为妻子)的方式看待其证据(Bigg & Suzi)。丈夫虽力陈妻子证据不可信,但未能证明其证据即便取最高也不能被接纳;若其证据被接纳,妻子即属在时限内起诉。故法院不满足于妻子无合理前景,驳回即决驳回申请——终止日期之争应在双方互相质证、全面审理后裁断。

单独审理:丈夫获准口头申请依规则第 10.10 条就「终止日期」单独审理。法院依 Marley & Ormonde(援 Southwell v Bennett)、Osferatu & Lawrenz 所列考量指出:一切事实与法律问题原则上应一并审理,除非单独审理「公正且便利」;且若涉证人可信度,通常不宜单独审理。本案「终止日期」之争并不窄——牵涉双方自 2017/2018 至 2021 年间的众多贡献,与第 44(6) 条许可所需考量高度重叠;无双方事实合意、同批证人将在两阶段重复作证、可信度须重复判定、并有作出矛盾认定与上诉之风险。表面诱人,实则徒增枝节;法院不信其能显著省费、收窄争点或提前结案,故驳回。

其余命令:丈夫 2026 年 3 月 31 日的程序申请因相关聆讯已被取消而失去实益,予以驳回;第 90RD 条宣告与(如需)第 44(6) 条许可,将与实体救济一并审理。就推进第 7 章单一专家证据:授权妻子代丈夫签署法务会计专家 Mr Box 与估价专家 Mr Bird 的聘任函,命丈夫于 2026 年 5 月 14 日前向 Mr Box 提交所需资料(已逾四月),逾期则其实体答辩被剔除、妻子可按无争辩方式进行。妻子要求丈夫预付 52,800 澳元专家费一节,因与诉讼资助命令之变更/执行属同类问题,予以无损驳回(留待另案)。各方费用保留。
html.en:
This is a procedural ruling within a de facto property proceeding. The de facto wife, Ms Naumov, commenced proceedings on 20 December 2023 seeking property adjustment under s 90SM and maintenance under s 90SE of the Family Law Act; the de facto husband, Mr Geissler, responded seeking dismissal and contending the wife needed leave to commence out of time under s 44(6). At the core is a dispute about when the de facto relationship ended: the wife says final separation in December 2021, the husband says it ended in May 2017 or March 2018.

On 30 January 2026 the husband filed an application seeking: a finding that final separation was 29 May 2017; dismissal of the wife's proceedings; and a s 90RD declaration that the parties were not in a de facto relationship thereafter (so the court would lack jurisdiction). He argued the termination date was a threshold jurisdictional question and 'dispositive' of the whole case — if it ended in 2017/2018, the wife's claim was out of time and could not continue absent s 44(6) leave. This was understood as seeking summary judgment.

Campton J's approach. Summary dismissal: the test is 'no reasonable prospects of success' (s 102QAB, r 10.09); the court does not resolve disputed facts but takes the case of the party resisting dismissal (the wife) at its highest (Bigg & Suzi). Though the husband pressed that the wife's evidence was not credible, he failed to establish that her evidence, taken at its highest, could not be accepted; if accepted, she had commenced within time. The court was not satisfied she had no reasonable prospects, and dismissed the application — the termination date should be decided after each party tests the other's evidence at a full hearing.

Separate hearing: the husband was granted leave to apply orally under r 10.10 for a separate hearing on the termination date. Per Marley & Ormonde (citing Southwell v Bennett) and Osferatu & Lawrenz, all questions of fact and law should generally be heard together unless a separate determination is 'just and convenient', and where witness credit is involved a separate trial is usually inappropriate. Here the termination dispute was not narrow — it engaged numerous contributions over 2017/2018 to 2021, overlapping heavily with what a s 44(6) leave application would require; there was no agreed statement of facts, the same witnesses would be recalled at both stages, credit would have to be decided twice, with a risk of inconsistent findings and appeals. Superficially attractive, it would only multiply complications; the court was not satisfied it would significantly save costs, narrow issues or hasten disposal, and dismissed it.

Other orders: the husband's 31 March 2026 procedural application had no utility once the relevant listing was vacated and was dismissed; the s 90RD declaration and any s 44(6) leave will be determined with the substantive relief. To progress the Ch 7 single-expert evidence: the wife was empowered to execute the engagement letters of forensic accountant Mr Box and valuer Mr Bird on the husband's behalf, and the husband was ordered to provide Mr Box's requested documents by 14 May 2026 (having had four months), failing which his substantive response is struck out and the wife may proceed undefended. The wife's request that the husband pre-pay $52,800 in expert fees was dismissed without prejudice (being of the same character as varying/enforcing the litigation-funding order, left to a separate application). All parties' costs were reserved.

html.zh:
性质:即席(ex tempore)程序裁定——丈夫的即决驳回/单独审理申请,加上推进专家证据的程序命令,由 Campton 法官于 2026 年 4 月 14 日经 Webex 裁定(地处 Sydney 登记区)。三名非事实关系答辩人(丈夫之姊及两家公司)就此提交了顺从通知(submitting notices)。

时间线:此前已有三份判决(2025 FedCFamC1F 132、612;2026 FedCFamC1F 45)→ 2023-12-20 妻子起诉(2025-03-31 修订)→ 2024-07-09 案件移交第一分庭 → 2025-09-03 追加丈夫之姊与两公司、并作出限制处分及诉讼资助命令 → 2025-12-01 任命第 7 章单一专家、并准丈夫于 2026-01-30 前提即决驳回申请 → 丈夫改提第 90RD 宣告申请(未依命令)→ 2026-04-14 本裁定。
html.en:
Nature: an ex tempore procedural ruling — the husband's summary-dismissal/separate-issues applications, plus procedural orders advancing the expert evidence — decided by Campton J on 14 April 2026 by Webex (Sydney Registry). The three non-relationship respondents (the husband's sister and two companies) filed submitting notices.

Timeline: three earlier judgments ([2025] FedCFamC1F 132 and 612; [2026] FedCFamC1F 45) → 20 Dec 2023, the wife commenced (amended 31 Mar 2025) → 9 Jul 2024, transferred to Division 1 → 3 Sep 2025, the husband's sister and two companies joined, with restraints on disposal and a litigation-funding order → 1 Dec 2025, Ch 7 single experts appointed and the husband given leave to file a summary-dismissal application by 30 Jan 2026 → the husband instead filed a s 90RD declaration application (not in compliance) → 14 Apr 2026, this decision.

框架
即决驳回的依据为《家庭法法》第 102QAB 条与《家事法规则》第 10.09 条(无合理胜诉前景即可驳回),并与《联邦巡回与家事法院法》第 46 条相呼应。第 102QAB(3) 条厘清「无合理前景」不等于「无望/注定败诉」,举证责任在申请方(第 102QAB(2)(b) 条)。单独审理依规则第 10.10、10.11 条(法院就可处分全/部分程序、缩短审理或省费之争点可单独裁断)。实体上:第 90SM 条(事实婚财产调整)、第 90SE 条(扶养);第 44(6) 条(逾时起诉的许可);第 90RD 条(就事实关系存否/终止作宣告)。

Framework
Summary dismissal rests on s 102QAB of the Family Law Act and r 10.09 (proceedings with no reasonable prospects of success may be dismissed), mirrored by s 46 of the FCFCOA Act. Section 102QAB(3) makes clear that "no reasonable prospects" does not mean "hopeless or bound to fail", and the onus is on the applicant (s 102QAB(2)(b)). Separate determination is under rr 10.10 and 10.11 (the court may separately decide an issue that could dispose of all or part of a proceeding, shorten a trial or save costs). On the merits: s 90SM (de facto property adjustment), s 90SE (maintenance); s 44(6) (leave to commence out of time); s 90RD (a declaration as to the existence or termination of a de facto relationship).

html.zh: 法院援引三件判例。Bigg & Suzi(家事法,1998)确立即决驳回中法院不解决争议事实、而以最有利于答方的方式看待其证据。Marley & Ormonde(2020,McClelland 副首席法官,转引 Southwell v Bennett)列举是否单独审理某争点的考量:谨慎为本、须落实立法的总体目的、原则上一并审理、须「公正且便利」、涉证人可信度时不宜单独审。Southwell v Bennett(新州最高法院,2010)即上述考量之出处。Osferatu & Lawrenz(2024,Behrens 法官)就单独审理列出大体相近的考量。
html.en: Four authorities feature. Bigg & Suzi (Family Court, 1998) establishes that on summary dismissal the court does not resolve disputed facts but takes the case of the party resisting at its highest. Marley & Ormonde (2020, McClelland DCJ, citing Southwell v Bennett) sets out the considerations for ordering a separate determination: approach with caution, give effect to the overriding purpose, generally hear all issues together, the moving party must show it is 'just and convenient', and a separate trial is inappropriate where witness credit is involved. Southwell v Bennett (NSW Supreme Court, 2010) is the source of those considerations. Osferatu & Lawrenz (2024, Behrens J) identifies not dissimilar considerations for a separate hearing.

html.zh:
1. 即决驳回的标准 —— 程序若无合理胜诉前景方可即决驳回(第 102QAB 条、规则第 10.09(1)(d) 条);第 102QAB(3) 条厘清「无合理前景」不等于「无望/注定败诉」,举证责任在丈夫;法院不解决争议事实,而以最有利于妻子的方式看待其证据(Bigg & Suzi)。

2. 「决定性」之主张不被接受 —— 丈夫称终止日期为门槛管辖问题、对全案具决定性。法院接受:若终止于 2017/2018,妻子确需第 44(6) 条许可;但不接受此即「决定性」——因妻子已明确,如未获 2021 年终止之认定,将退而申请逾时许可。

3. 即决驳回——驳回 —— 丈夫虽力陈妻子证据不可信,但未能证明其证据即便取最高也不能被接纳;若被接纳,妻子即在时限内起诉。法院不满足于妻子无合理胜诉前景。终止日期之争应在双方互相质证、全面审理后裁断,故驳回即决驳回申请。

4. 单独审理的标准 —— 依 Marley & Ormonde(援 Southwell v Bennett)及 Osferatu & Lawrenz:单独审理权应审慎行使、落实总体目的;一切事实法律问题原则上一并审理,除非「公正且便利」且能更快更省地解决;涉证人可信度时不宜单独审。

5. 单独审理——驳回 —— 「终止日期」之争并不窄:牵涉双方自 2017/2018 至 2021 年间众多贡献,与第 44(6) 条许可考量高度重叠;无事实合意、同批证人将在两阶段重复作证、可信度须重复判定、并有矛盾认定与上诉之风险。表面诱人,实则徒增枝节;法院不信其能显著省费、收窄争点或提前结案,故驳回。

6. 其余处理 —— 丈夫 2026-03-31 程序申请已失实益,予以驳回;第 90RD 宣告与(如需)第 44(6) 许可将与实体救济一并审理。推进单一专家证据:丈夫未指明拒签聘任函有何不利,授权妻子代签;丈夫已逾四月未交资料,命其于 2026-05-14 前向 Mr Box 提交,逾期则实体答辩被剔除、妻子可无争辩进行。妻子要求预付 52,800 澳元专家费一节,与诉讼资助命令之变更/执行属同类,予以无损驳回(留待另案);费用保留。
html.en:
1. The summary-dismissal test — proceedings may be summarily dismissed only if they have no reasonable prospects of success (s 102QAB; r 10.09(1)(d)); s 102QAB(3) clarifies 'no reasonable prospects' does not mean 'hopeless or bound to fail', the onus is on the husband, and the court does not resolve disputed facts but takes the wife's case at its highest (Bigg & Suzi).

2. The 'dispositive' contention rejected — the husband said the termination date was a threshold jurisdictional question, dispositive of the case. The court accepted that if it ended in 2017/2018 the wife would need s 44(6) leave; but it did not accept this was 'dispositive' — the wife had made clear that, failing a 2021 finding, she would seek leave to commence out of time.

3. Summary dismissal — refused — although the husband pressed that the wife's evidence was not credible, he failed to establish that her evidence, taken at its highest, could not be accepted; if accepted, she had commenced within time. The court was not satisfied she had no reasonable prospects. The termination date should be decided after each party tests the other's evidence at a full hearing, so the application was dismissed.

4. The separate-hearing test — per Marley & Ormonde (citing Southwell v Bennett) and Osferatu & Lawrenz: the power should be exercised with caution and to give effect to the overriding purpose; all questions of fact and law should generally be heard together unless it is 'just and convenient' and would resolve matters more quickly and cheaply; a separate trial is inappropriate where witness credit is involved.

5. Separate hearing — refused — the termination dispute was not narrow: it engaged numerous contributions over 2017/2018 to 2021, overlapping heavily with s 44(6) leave considerations; there was no agreed statement of facts, the same witnesses would be recalled at both stages, credit would be decided twice, with a risk of inconsistent findings and appeals. Superficially attractive, it would only multiply complications; the court was not satisfied it would significantly save costs, narrow issues or hasten disposal, and dismissed it.

6. The balance — the husband's 31 March 2026 procedural application had lost utility and was dismissed; the s 90RD declaration and any s 44(6) leave will be determined with the substantive relief. To progress the single-expert evidence: as the husband identified no prejudice in executing the engagement letters, the wife was empowered to execute them; having had four months, the husband was ordered to provide Mr Box's documents by 14 May 2026, failing which his substantive response is struck out and the wife may proceed undefended. The wife's request that the husband pre-pay $52,800 in expert fees, being of the same character as varying/enforcing the litigation-funding order, was dismissed without prejudice (left to a separate application); costs were reserved.

html.zh:
裁判要旨

即决驳回的门槛极高:须以最有利于答方的方式看待其证据,只有在其确无合理胜诉前景(并非仅「可能败诉」)时方可准予;举证责任在申请人,法院不在此阶段解决争议事实。

单独审理某争点须审慎:原则上一切事实法律问题应一并审理,除非单独审理「公正且便利」、能确实更快更省地解决;当争点不窄、与其他问题事实重叠、且涉证人可信度须重复判定时,单独审理多半弊大于利,不应作出。
html.en:
Ratio

The threshold for summary dismissal is high: the resisting party's evidence is taken at its highest, and dismissal follows only where there are truly no reasonable prospects of success (not merely a likelihood of losing); the onus is on the applicant, and the court does not resolve disputed facts at this stage.

Ordering a separate determination calls for caution: all questions of fact and law should generally be heard together unless a separate hearing is 'just and convenient' and would genuinely resolve matters more quickly and cheaply; where the issue is not narrow, overlaps factually with others, and would require witness credit to be decided more than once, a separate trial usually does more harm than good and should not be ordered.

html.zh:
Summary dismissal · 即决驳回
在无合理胜诉前景时不经全面审理即驳回程序(第 102QAB 条)(Summary dismissal: dismissing a proceeding without a full hearing where it has no reasonable prospects of success (s 102QAB)).
Taken at its highest · 取其最高
在即决阶段以最有利于答方的方式看待其证据(Taken at its highest: assessing the resisting party's evidence in the way most favourable to them at the summary stage).
Separate issues hearing · 单独争点审理
就某一争点先行、单独裁断的程序(规则第 10.10 条)(Separate issues hearing: determining one issue separately and in advance (r 10.10)).
De facto relationship · 事实婚关系
未登记婚姻但共同生活的伴侣关系;其存否/终止可依第 90RD 条宣告(De facto relationship: a couple living together without marriage; its existence/termination may be declared under s 90RD).
s 44(6) leave · 第 44(6) 条许可
逾两年时限提起财产/扶养程序所需的法院许可(s 44(6) leave: the court's permission to commence property/maintenance proceedings outside the two-year limit).
s 90RD declaration · 第 90RD 条宣告
法院就是否存在或何时终止事实关系所作的宣告(s 90RD declaration: a court declaration as to whether, or when, a de facto relationship existed or ended).
Ch 7 single expert · 第 7 章单一专家
由各方共用、为审判提供意见的单一鉴定专家(Ch 7 single expert: a single jointly-used expert appointed to give opinion evidence for trial).
html.en:
Summary dismissal
Dismissing a proceeding without a full hearing where it has no reasonable prospects of success (s 102QAB).
Taken at its highest
Assessing the resisting party's evidence in the way most favourable to them at the summary stage.
Separate issues hearing
Determining one issue separately and in advance (r 10.10).
De facto relationship
A couple living together without marriage; its existence/termination may be declared under s 90RD.
s 44(6) leave
The court's permission to commence property/maintenance proceedings outside the two-year limit.
s 90RD declaration
A court declaration as to whether, or when, a de facto relationship existed or ended.
Ch 7 single expert
A single jointly-used expert appointed to give opinion evidence for trial.

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

These reasons determine the Applications in a Proceeding of Mr Geissler (“the de facto husband”) filed on 30 January 2026 as sealed on 2 February 2026 and 31 March 2026 as sealed on 2 April 2026 and the Responses to an Application in a Proceeding of Ms Naumov (“the de facto wife”) each filed on 9 April 2026.

Each of the three non de facto relationship respondents, Ms Geissler, S2 Pty Ltd and Geissler Nominees Pty Ltd, filed Submitting Notices on 10 April 2026 as to the relief sought by the de facto husband for summary determination of the substantive relief as sought by the de facto wife and as to his application seeking other procedural orders. They have reserved their position in relation to contentions recently made by the de facto wife as identified later in these reasons.

These reasons assume familiarity with the judgments delivered on 28 February 2025 (Naumov & Geissler [2025] FedCFamC1F 132), 3 September 2025 (Naumov & Geissler (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 612) and 30 January 2026 (Naumov & Geissler (No 3) [2026] FedCFamC1F 45).

BACKGROUND

To give context to the Applications in a Proceeding filed by the de facto husband, the reasons for judgment delivered on 3 September 2025 record:

The de facto wife contends that she and the de facto husband commenced cohabitation in [...], separated on 29 May 2017 under the same roof and resumed cohabitation shortly, thereafter, separated for a further month on 13 March 2018, again resuming cohabitation, and affected a final separation [in] December 2021.

The de facto husband contends that the de facto relationship terminated either in May 2017 or March 2018.

It is uncontroversial that, from the de facto husband’s contended dates of separation, until [...] December 2021, both he and the de facto wife continued to reside in a property at [E Street], [Suburb F] (“the [Suburb F] property”). That property is owned by the [Geissler Properties No 2 Unit Trust]. The de facto husband vacated the property [in December 2021] upon the making of an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (“ADVO”) in the [...] Local Court prohibiting and restricting his behaviour for the protection of the de facto wife and the two children of the relationship, [X] born [in 2009], and currently 16 years of age, and [Y] born in [in 2011] and currently 14 years of age. Both children have lived with the de facto wife at the [Suburb F] property since December 2021.

...

The de facto wife filed an Initiating Application seeking orders adjusting property between she and the de facto husband pursuant to s 90SM of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) on 15 March 2018 in SYC1624/2018. [Geissler Nominees Pty Ltd] and [S1 Pty Ltd] were also parties to the proceeding. The proceedings were discontinued on 27 March 2018 by way of a consent order entered by all parties. Solicitors acted for the de facto wife in the proceeding. The de facto husband, the second respondent, and the third respondents retained counsel to appear on their behalf for the purposes of entering consent orders for discontinuance.

Final ADVO orders were made restricting and prohibiting the de facto husband’s behaviour [in mid] 2023 for a period of two years (Exhibit 1). The orders include that he is to not come within 500 metres of the [Suburb F] property. The order expired [in mid] 2025. During the hearing, the de facto husband said that he appealed three “charges” from the Local Court determination, of which one was successful in March 2025, and the remaining two adjourned to an unknown date. He did not disclose any further information on that subject matter.

On 20 December 2023, the de facto wife again commenced proceedings by way of an Initiating Application in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) seeking orders for the adjustment of property between she and the de facto husband pursuant to s 90SM of the Act together with orders for her periodic maintenance. The de facto husband, in his Response to an Initiating Application filed on 19 June 2024, seeks that the de facto wife’s Initiating Application be dismissed and an order as to costs. The de facto husband contends that the de facto wife requires leave pursuant to s 44(6) of the Act to commence proceedings for the adjustment of property out of time. The proceedings were transferred to this Court on 9 July 2024. His sister, the second respondent, has not filed any documents in the proceeding.

The substantive relief agitated by the de facto wife is contained in her Amended Initiating Application filed on 31 March 2025. She seeks orders by way of property adjustment between she and the de facto husband pursuant to s 90SM of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) and an order for her own maintenance pursuant to s 90SE of the Act.

The de facto husband seeks by way of his Response to an Initiating Application filed on 19 June 2024 as amended on 3 December 2025 that the de facto wife’s substantive relief be dismissed.

On 9 July 2024, the proceeding was transferred to Division 1.

On 23 August 2024, consent orders were made that included:

In the first instance the de facto husband meet the cost of the ch 7 single expert valuers and the de facto wife shall account to the de facto husband for 50 per cent of that cost incurred within 21 days of the date of any final order made as to the adjustment property in these proceedings, or upon the conclusion of the proceedings, or at such other time as agreed by the parties in writing

On 3 September 2025, orders were made joining the de facto husband’s sister, Ms Geissler, and two corporations, S2 Pty Ltd and Geissler Nominees Pty Ltd, to the proceedings as the second, third and fourth respondents; restraining the de facto husband and the second respondent from disposing of property including E Street, Suburb F (“the [Suburb F] property”) occupied by the de facto wife and the children; and for litigation funding in favour of the de facto wife by way of a dollar-for-dollar order payable by the de facto husband.

On 11 November 2025, orders were made as to the identification of requisite ch 7 single expert evidence for the purposes of trial.

Orders made on 1 December 2025 provided for the appointment of ch 7 single experts to opine as to the value of property. The following further orders were made:

On or before 30 January 2026, the de facto husband has leave to file and serve an Application in a Proceeding seeking orders for the summary dismissal of the substantive application of the de facto wife and as to an injunction restraining the current legal representatives of the de facto wife continuing to act in the proceeding, together with an affidavit in support thereof.

Within 28 days of the filing of any Application in a Proceeding pursuant to Order 6 hereof, the de facto wife, and should they consider it relevant, the second to fourth respondents, shall file and serve any response to the Application in a Proceeding, and affidavit material in support thereof.

The hearing of the husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed pursuant to Order 6 hereof will be listed on 48 hours’ notice to the parties by way of orders issued from chambers.

(Emphasis in original)

On 2 February 2026, notations and orders were made in chambers as follows:

THE COURT NOTES THAT:

Pursuant to Order 6 made on 1 December 2025, the de facto husband was granted leave to file and serve an Application in a Proceeding seeking orders for the summary dismissal of the substantive application of the de facto wife and as to an injunction restraining the current legal representatives of the de facto wife continuing to act in the proceeding, together with an affidavit in support thereof.

On 30 January 2026, the de facto husband filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking s 90RD declarations. That application was not filed in compliance with Order 6 made on 1 December 2025.

THE COURT ORDERS THAT:

The Application in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 30 January 2026 is listed for mention at 9.30 am on 24 April 2026.

Insofar as is necessary, the operation of Orders 7 and 8 made on 1 December 2025 is suspended.

(Bold emphasis added)

On 1 April 2026, the following further orders were made in chambers:

On or before 9 April 2026, each of the de facto wife and the second to fourth respondents shall file and serve:

(a) A Response to the Application in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 30 January 2026 and any affidavit material in support thereof; and

(b) A Response to the Application in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 31 March 2026 and any affidavit in support thereof.

The listing of the Application in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 30 January 2026 for mention on 24 April 2026 is vacated.

The Application(s) in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 30 January 2026 and 31 March 2026 are listed for summary determination and/or hearing before Justice Campton on 14 April 2026 at 3.15 pm by Webex.

(Emphasis in original)

THE COMPETING RELIEF SOUGHT

The de facto husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 30 January 2026, as sealed on 2 February 2026, seeks the following orders:

That the Court finds the parties date of separation on a final basis to be 29 May 2017.

That the Initiating Application filed 20 December 2023 and all applications before the court be dismissed.

Pursuant to s.90RD of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) ("the Act"), that the Court make a declaration that the parties were not in a de facto relationship after 29 May 2017 AND IT BE NOTED that the Court is therefore absent in jurisdiction to entertain any application under Part VI IIAB of the Act.

(As per the original)

The de facto husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 31 March 2026, as sealed on 2 April 2026, seeks the following orders:

That the case management hearing currently listed on 24 April 2026 be vacated.

That the matter be relisted for case management on the next available date convenient to the Court after 24 April 2026.

That the time for compliance with any existing procedural orders (including those relating to expert evidence) be extended accordingly.

That the costs of this application be reserved.

Liberty to apply on 3 days' notice.

The de facto wife’s Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 9 April 2026 seeks the following orders in response to the de facto husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 30 January 2026, as sealed on 2 February 2026:

That the First Respondent's application in a proceeding filed on 30 January 2026 be consolidated and heard with the substantive s 90SM claim.

That if the Court determines that the parties’ relationship ended on the day contended for by the First Respondent, then to the extent that it is necessary, leave be granted to the Applicant pursuant to s 44(6) of the Family Law Act 1975 to commence proceedings pursuant to s 90SM out of time and that such an application be consolidated and heard with the substantive s 90SM claim.

In the alternative, that the First Respondent's application in a proceeding filed on 30 January 2026 be dismissed.

(As per the original)

The de facto wife’s Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 9 April 2026 seeks the following orders in response to the de facto husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 31 March 2026, as sealed on 2 April 2026:

That the First Respondent's application in a proceeding filed on 31 March 2026 be dismissed.

2. That by no later than 4pm on 21 April 2026:

(a) The First Respondent sign and provide to Joseph Box (the forensic accountant expert) the signed engagement letter sent by the forensic accountant expert on 18 December 2025;

(b) The First Respondent provide to the forensic accountant expert all documents and information requested by the forensic accountant expert on 18 December 2025;

(c) The First Respondent confirm in writing to David Bird (the property valuation expert) that Mr Bird's terms and quotation letter sent on 6 March 2026 are accepted;

(d) The First Respondent make pre-payment of the property valuation expert fees in the amount of $52,800 (incl. GST) or any other amount acceptable for the property valuation expert to commence carry.[Qg out the valuations

That within 7 days of any request for documents, information, payment of the experts' fees and/or access to any of the properties the subject of the valuation by either the forensic accountant expert or the property valuation expert, the First Respondent is to provide to the experts all the documents, information, confirmation of payment of the experts' fees in full and/or access to any of the properties the subject of the valuation to the experts.

If the First Respondent fails or neglects to take any action in compliance with orders 2 and 3 hereof, leave is granted to the Applicant de facto wife to proceed with her s 90SM application as against the First Respondent on an undefended basis.

The proceedings are listed for further case management on a date suitable to the Court.

(As per the original)

CONSIDERATION

The de facto husband’s application for summary dismissal

The de facto husband confirmed that the relief agitated in paragraph 2 of his Application in a Proceeding filed on 30 January 2026 purports to seek summary judgment in his favour.

Proceedings may be summarily dismissed on the application of a party or on the court’s own initiative if they have no reasonable prospects of success (s 102QAB of the Act and r 10.09(1)(d) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”)). The rule supplements the legislative provision (see also s 46(2)(b) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) mirroring the provisions of s 102QAB of the Act).

Section 102QAB(3) defines “no reasonable prospects of success” as that “the proceedings need not be hopeless; or bound to fail”. The practical effect of the section is that the case as pleaded, including the evidence in support thereof, does not disclose a reasonable cause of action.

Pursuant to s 102QAB(2)(b), it is a matter for the de facto husband to establish that the de facto wife has “no reasonable prospect of successfully prosecuting the proceedings or that part of the proceedings”.

It is well established that a court hearing a claim for summary dismissal does not resolve disputed facts but takes the applicant de facto wife’s case at its highest (Bigg & Suzi [1998] FamCA 14; (1998) FLC 92-799). Hence, the de facto husband bears the onus of establishing, if the de facto wife’s evidence is taken at its highest, that she has no reasonable prospects of success in seeking orders for the division of property or orders as to her own maintenance.

The orders sought by the de facto husband in that Application in a Proceeding, as a matter of construction and from the content of his written submissions, pivots from his contention that “the central issue [in the proceeding] remains the date of separation, which is a threshold jurisdictional question”. He says he agitates a “threshold issue” as to whether the de facto wife’s claim for orders as to property settlement pursuant to s 90SM of the Act or her maintenance pursuant to s 90SE of the Act have been commenced within two years from the date of the termination of the relationship as prescribed by s 44(5)(a)(i) of the Act.

It is his contention that the determination of this factual dispute is “dispositive” of the proceeding because if the date of termination of the de facto relationship is found to be in either 2017 or 2018 (as asserted by him), “the proceedings cannot continue absent leave under s 44(6)” of the Act. This submission is correct as to the requirement for the de facto wife to achieve the indulgence of leave to commence out of time if the factual contest between the parties is determined as he contends it ought to be, but the submission is not accepted as being “dispositive” to the proceedings.

The de facto husband acknowledges in his written submissions filed on 14 April 2026, as previously recorded in Notation F to the orders made on 13 December 2024, that, if necessary, should a finding not be made as sought by the de facto wife that the de facto relationship terminated in December 2021, she will seek leave to commence s 90SM proceedings out of time pursuant to s 44(6) of the Act. In his written submissions he said:

A3. Leave pursuant to S44(6) of the Act

This is a clear admission by the Applicant that her Initiating Application dated 20 December 2023 is out of time

In circumstances where leave has not been granted, and an application seeking leave is not before this Court, it is unreasonable to expect the Respondent to turn his mind to the application including detailing prejudice to be experienced by the Respondent

However of significant important, is the the fact the Applicant was aware of the limitation period as she swore at [16] within affidavit dated 20 December 2023 – this would be difficult to reconcile in circumstances where the Applicant was actively engaging with lawyers 2 months after her asserted date of separation

On that note, the Applicant’s submissions at paragraph 20 to 28 have no purpose in these submissions and the Court ought to reject them in full

(As per the original)

The de facto wife submitted in her Case Outline filed on 13 April 2026:

3. ...that in respect of Declarations Application, the Court should:

dismiss the Declarations Application on a summary basis, as the appropriate course is for any matter arising under s 90RD of the Act should be determined at trial;

alternatively, if the Court is minded to determine Declarations Application, to dismiss the Declarations Application on the available evidence demonstrating that the relationship in accordance with the standards that apply to summary dismissal applications;

alternatively, if the Court is minded to find that separation occurred on 29 May 2017, the Court would grant leave under s 44(6) of the Act to commence the s 90SM proceeding out of time.

The Response to an Application in a Proceeding of the de facto wife filed on 9 April 2026 now formally seeks this relief understood to be sought by her in the alternative without concession to her contended date of termination.

The de facto husband’s written submissions filed on 14 April 2026 are for the large part directed as to why a finding of fact ought to be made that the de facto relationship terminated either on 29 May 2017 or alternatively on 13 March 2018. His submissions highlight on a summary basis what he says are admissions and reconstructions concluded from his interpretations of the de facto wife’s prior and current evidence; from asserted inconsistencies in that evidence; from the acceptance of his evidence of “cohabitation and lifestyle evidence”; and his evidence of “commercial reality”, together with other issues as to the credibility of the de facto wife’s evidence.

The de facto husband’s oral submissions in support of his relief for summary dismissal of the de facto wife’s substantive relief are indicative of the absence of merit to his application for summary determination. He broadly asserts, absent the testing of the de facto wife’s evidence as to the date of termination of the relationship, her evidence “cannot be preferred”. While he valiantly attempted to contend that her evidence was inherently unbelievable or contrary to compelling inferences, he has failed to establish that the de facto wife’s evidence cannot be open to acceptance at its highest. In the event her evidence is accepted on that basis, she has commenced her substantive relief within time.

I am not satisfied the de facto husband has established that the de facto wife has no reasonable prospects of success, should her evidence be accepted at its highest, of achieving a finding that termination of the de facto relationship between the parties occurred in December 2021.

Self-evidently, this factual contest ought to be determined after each party has had the opportunity to test the evidence of the other party and there has been a fulsome consideration of all other evidence relevant to that issue, together with submissions. The de facto husband’s application for summary dismissal as asserted to be contained in paragraph 2 of his Application in a Proceeding filed on 30 January 2026, as sealed on 2 February 2026, will be dismissed.

The application for a separate issues hearing

The content of the de facto husband’s written submissions filed on 14 April 2026 developed so that he was granted leave to make an oral application for the listing of a separate issues hearing pursuant to r 10.10 of the Rules as to the date of termination of the de facto relationship pursuant to s 90RD of the Act, as sought in paragraph 3 of his Application in a Proceeding filed on 30 January 2026 and sealed on 2 February 2026.

The de facto husband broadly submits that such a separate issues hearing would promote the interests of justice by way of a “just, quick and inexpensive resolution” of the proceeding, deferring or avoiding unnecessary procedural steps and not exposing the de facto relationship parties and the other parties to disproportionate costs.

Rule 10.10 of the Rules provides that:

(1) A party may apply for a decision on any issue, if the decision may:

(a) dispose of all or part of the proceeding; or

(b) make a trial unnecessary; or

(c) make a trial substantially shorter; or

(d) save substantial costs.

(2) An application under this rule must be made by filing an application in accordance with the approved form.

Rule 10.11 of the Rules further provides that:

(1) On an application under this Part, the court may:

(a) dismiss any part of the proceeding; or

(b) decide an issue; or

(c) make a final order on any issue; or

(d) order a hearing about an issue or fact; or

(e) with the consent of the parties, order arbitration about the proceeding or a part of the proceeding.

(2) If the court makes orders against a party who is claiming relief against the party who obtains the orders, the court may stay execution on, or other enforcement of, the orders until determination of that claim.

For the purposes of r 10.11, the word “issue” may be an issue of fact or an issue of law. The construction of the rule is broad, providing the Court with a wide discretion to utilise the powers identified in the rule with reference to the Act.

In Marley & Ormonde [2020] FamCA 1046, McClelland DCJ set out the considerations to be taken into account in determining whether to make an order for a separate determination of an issue, as summarised by Hallen AsJ (as his Honour then was) in Southwell v Bennett [2010] NSWSC 1372 at [15]–[17]. As cited by the de facto wife in her Case Outline filed on 13 April 2026, Behrens J identified not dissimilar considerations in Osferatu & Lawrenz [2024] FedCFamC1F 696.

To my mind, the following considerations are relevant in the determination of the oral application of the de facto husband:

(a) As a general rule, the discretionary power to order a separate determination of a question should be approached with caution.

(b) In exercising its discretion, the overriding purpose of the Act and the Rules must be given effect.

(c) Generally, all questions of fact and law should be determined at the one time. If the Court is to depart from that position, the party seeking the separate determination of a question must satisfy the Court that it would be “just and convenient” for that order to be made.

(d) Before a question is to be separately determined, it must be possible to clearly see that it will facilitate the quicker and cheaper resolution of the proceedings.

(e) Where findings as to the credit of a witness or witnesses is, or may be, involved in the consideration of the evidence relevant to the question, it is inappropriate to order a separate trial.

I find the starting position in this contest is that all questions of fact and law should be heard and finally determined together, unless there is a good reason to order that the separate determination of a particular issue would give effect to the overarching purpose of the Rules and the Act.

The Court’s task in determining whether such warrant exists in this matter requires balancing considerations as to whether the separate listing will achieve a just determination in the circumstances of the case, efficiently using the resources of the Court so as to dispose of the proceedings in a timely manner at a financial and resource-based cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.

The separate issue as to the determination of the factual contest as to the date of termination of the de facto relationship is not narrow. It engages directly with a consideration of a myriad of contributions made by each of the de facto parties over the period from 2017 or 2018 up until the date of contended separation as asserted by the de facto wife in 2021 while both parties occupied the same home, and continuing contributions thereafter, each being relevant in a not dissimilar way to any application, if necessary, for leave pursuant to s 44(6) of the Act. This evidence will engage with consideration as to the balance of convenience by way of the case asserted by the de facto wife and the contentions as to hardship asserted by each party over the same period.

There is an overlapping issue as to questions of fact and law in relation to each of those different proposed separate hearings in the proceedings.

There is no agreed statement of facts between the parties for the purposes of a separate hearing. It is likely that the same witnesses will be called at both stages of the possible hearings of the proceedings, leading to a risk of possible inconsistent findings arising from determinations of separate questions. It is plain that issues as to the credit of those witnesses will be required to be determined for the proposed separate issues trial, and any subsequent relief pursuant to s 44(6) if necessary, and also as to any substantive relief as to property division and maintenance.

Hence, while it may appear superficially attractive to order a separate trial for a s 90RD declaration on the question of the date of termination of the de facto relationship, it will almost certainly produce consequential complications, including overlapping factual issues, multiple questions as to credibility, and as to potential appeals. It is evident that the same witnesses are likely to give the same evidence of questions to be separately tried and those questions in themselves may produce risks of inconsistent findings.

I am not satisfied that the de facto husband has established that there would be a significant saving of costs and/or a substantial narrowing of issues, or that it would even lead to the earlier disposal of the proceedings, if the separate issues trial was determined as he seeks. The de facto husband’s oral application for a separate issues trial as to the date of termination of the de facto relationship will be dismissed.

The Application in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 31 March 2026 and sealed 2 April 2026

The de facto husband agreed in circumstances where the chambers order made on 1 April 2026 vacated the listing on 24 April 2026, that his Application in a Proceeding filed on 31 March 2026 and sealed on 2 April 2026 no longer had utility and would be dismissed. Such order will be made.

Procedurally consolidating outstanding prayers for relief not determined

For all of the above reasons, an order will be made that any declaration sought by a party as to the date of the termination of the de facto relationship pursuant to s 90RD of the Act, and any application for the indulgence of leave pursuant to s 44(6) of the Act to commence proceedings out of time and for adjustment pursuant to s 90SM or s 90SE of the Act (if required) will be determined at the same time as the substantive relief as sought by the de facto husband and the de facto wife in the financial proceedings.

The relief sought by the wife to progress ch 7 single expert evidence

The de facto wife contends that notwithstanding the history identified earlier in these reasons, the de facto husband has failed to execute and return to the ch 7 single forensic accounting expert, Mr Joseph Box, his letter of engagement dated 17 December 2025, and has failed or neglected to execute and return to the ch 7 single real property valuation expert, Mr David Bird, his letter of engagement dated 6 March 2026.

The de facto husband says he has signed letters of engagement to “experts” but cannot recall the identity of those experts. He said it was his proposal to “pause the experts” until his summary application was determined.

In circumstances where the de facto husband does not identify any prejudice in execution of the letters to the ch 7 single forensic accounting expert, Mr Box, and the ch 7 single real property valuation expert, Mr Bird, an order will be made for the de facto wife to execute and return the letters of instruction to the experts.

Mr Box, the ch 7 single forensic accounting expert, under cover of his letter directed to the parties dated 17 December 2025, sought documents and information currently within the province of the de facto husband. The de facto husband is currently outside the jurisdiction, returning on 24 April 2026. He has not provided the requested information and documents to Mr Box. It is necessary to progress the proceedings. He has now had four months to collate that material. An order will be made for the de facto husband to provide the documents to the ch 7 single forensic accounting expert under cover of his letter dated 17 December 2025 on or before 14 May 2026.

Pre-payment by the de facto husband of ch 7 single expert fees

Order 10 made on 23 August 2024 provides that in the first instance the de facto husband meet the cost of the ch 7 single expert valuers and the de facto wife shall account to the de facto husband for 50 per cent of that cost incurred within 21 days of the date of any final orders made as to the adjustment of property in these proceedings, or upon the conclusion of the proceedings, or at such other time as agreed by the parties in writing.

By way of her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 9 April 2026, the de facto wife sought an order that the de facto husband pre-pay the ch 7 single experts’ fees in the sum of $52,800. At the hearing she could not identify a ready source of those funds to be pre-paid by the de facto husband, notwithstanding the de facto husband is currently undertaking business class travel to and from Europe.

The de facto wife has identified that she proposes to make a further application for the variation, discharge, or enforcement of the dollar-for-dollar order and litigation funding order made on 3 September 2025 payable by the de facto husband.

To my mind, at this point in the proceedings, the relief sought as to pre-payment of ch 7 single expert fees and the enforcement of or variation of litigation funding broadly falls within the same parameters engaging not dissimilar considerations. In those circumstances, the relief sought by the de facto wife in paragraph 2(d) in her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 9 April 2026 will be dismissed without prejudice to her to file such Application in a Proceeding as to these subject matters as she is advised.

No opportunity was afforded to the de facto husband or the de facto wife to agitate costs of and incidental to the competing applications determined today. An order will be made that the costs of all parties of and incidental to the Applications in a Proceeding and Responses thereto determined today will be reserved.

For the sake of completeness and to avoid further disputes, an order will be made that the Application in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 30 January 2026, sealed on 2 February 2026 (specifically except as to paragraph 3 as to a s 90RD declaration as to the date of termination of the de facto relationship) and the Application in a Proceeding of the de facto husband filed on 31 March 2026, sealed on 2 April 2026 and the Responses to the Applications in a Proceeding of the de facto wife each filed on 9 April 2026 (specifically except as to paragraph 2 as to leave if necessary to commence Pt VIIIAB proceedings out of time pursuant to s 44(6) of the Act) be dismissed.

For all the above reasons, I make the orders as set out herein.

NOTE:

These reasons have been corrected from the transcript. Topic headings have been inserted, and grammatical errors have been corrected. In addition, amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clear and easy to read.
